does: This paper introduces SAERec, a recommendation system that automatically constructs a fine-grained, interpretable intent space by applying sparse autoencoders to extract intents from textual embeddings rather than directly from user sequences. The method uses sparse autoencoders to disentangle and interpret latent dimensions in large language model text embeddings, creating explicit semantic intent priors that guide recommendation. The approach treats textual corpora as high-information-density evidence for intent discovery, avoiding the need to manually preset the number of intents or rely on sequence clustering. By grounding intents in LLM embeddings through interpretable features, SAERec generates more complete and semantically meaningful intent sets that improve both recommendation accuracy and system interpretability.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature of intent-based recommender systems that seek to improve accuracy and explainability by modeling underlying user motivations. It extends prior clustering and prototype-learning approaches that derive intents directly from user interaction sequences, responding to their limitations around sequence quality sensitivity and preset intent cardinality. The paper leverages recent advances in mechanistic interpretability of neural networks, particularly sparse autoencoders used to decompose latent representations into interpretable features. It also capitalizes on the emergence of large language models as sources of rich semantic representations. By combining sparse autoencoder interpretability techniques with textual grounding in LLM embeddings, SAERec synthesizes insights from interpretability research and semantic-aware recommendation systems.
enables: This work opens pathways for more principled, data-driven construction of interpretable recommendation systems through neural network interpretability techniques. The sparse autoencoder approach to discovering intent priors from LLM embeddings could extend to other recommendation domains and modalities where textual descriptions exist. The method enables future research into how interpretable latent dimensions discovered by SAEs can improve explainability in recommendations and help users understand why specific items are suggested. Additionally, the framework supports investigating the relationship between mechanistic features in language models and real-world user intents, potentially advancing both recommendation and interpretability research. This could inform better interaction between structured intent priors and sequence modeling, and inspire applications in personalization beyond recommendations.
